posts.hook: 1 in 10 people has this vision problem. It's been misdiagnosed as ADHD for decades.
posts.caption:
Convergence Insufficiency.

The eyes fail to work together at close range.

Symptoms:
- Words move or blur while reading
- Losing your place on a line
- Headaches after 20 minutes of reading
- Double vision at close range

1 in 10 people.

Most are diagnosed with reading difficulties, ADHD, or dyslexia instead.

Free convergence test: axeo.vision

#ConvergenceInsufficiency #EyeHealth #ADHD #Axeo #HealthTok #VisionTest #ReadingProblems

posts.hook: What convergence insufficiency actually is — and the exercise optometrists prescribe.
posts.caption:
Your eyes are supposed to converge — aim at the same point.

In convergence insufficiency, the eyes drift outward. To read, they have to work overtime to stay aligned. Eventually they give up.

Result: double vision, blur, headaches, losing place while reading.

The treatment: convergence exercises.

Most prescribed: pencil push-ups.
1. Hold pencil at arm's length.
2. Focus on the tip.
3. Slowly bring toward your nose.
4. Stop at the first sign of doubling.
5. Push back slowly. Repeat 10x.

Guided version at axeo.vision/exercise

#ConvergenceInsufficiency #EyeExercise #Axeo #AxeoVision #VisionTherapy #Optometry #ReadingDifficulty

posts.hook: The vision problem that affects 1 in 10 — and hides behind ADHD diagnoses.
posts.caption:
Convergence insufficiency has been called 'the hidden reading disorder'.

→ 10% of the population
→ Frequently misdiagnosed as ADHD or dyslexia
→ Children: often show behavioral problems in class (not paying attention = eye fatigue)
→ Adults: reduced reading stamina, headaches after screen work

The fix: a few minutes of targeted eye exercises per day.

Guidance at axeo.vision/exercise — free, browser-based.

#ConvergenceInsufficiency #EyeHealth #Axeo #AxeoVision #ReadingDifficulty #ADHD #VisionTherapy

posts.hook: Children labeled 'lazy readers' may actually have an eye tracking problem.
posts.caption:
Convergence insufficiency is one of the top undiagnosed causes of reading difficulties in children.

What teachers and parents see:
- Child avoids reading
- Loses place constantly
- Complains of headaches after school
- Seems inattentive (it's actually eye fatigue)
- Covers one eye while reading (compensating)

What's actually happening: the eyes drift apart during close work. Keeping them aligned is exhausting.

The diagnosis: a simple near point of convergence test.
The treatment: office-based vision therapy + home exercises.

Children improve significantly in 12–16 weeks of structured treatment.

Free awareness exercises: axeo.vision/exercise

#ChildEyeHealth #ConvergenceInsufficiency #LearningDifficulties #EyeHealth #Axeo #Optometry

posts.hook: Convergence insufficiency — the eye problem that gets kids misdiagnosed with ADHD. A thread:
posts.caption:
1/ 1 in 10 people has convergence insufficiency (CI). The eyes fail to aim at the same close-up point, causing blur, doubling, and fatigue during reading.

2/ The symptoms in children look exactly like inattention: losing place while reading, avoiding books, frequent headaches after school, covering one eye, tilting the head.

3/ A JAMA study found that children with ADHD symptoms showed significantly higher rates of CI than controls. In many cases, treating CI improved attention and reading scores.

4/ CI is NOT detected by a standard 20/20 eye test. It requires a near point of convergence measurement — a 2-minute test most routine eye exams skip.

5/ Treatment: office-based vision therapy (12–16 weekly sessions) is the gold standard. Home-based pencil push-ups have weaker evidence but are widely prescribed as support.

6/ Axeo has guided convergence exercises at axeo.vision/exercise — free. Includes pencil push-up guide, convergence tracking, and difficulty progression.

Share this with any parent worried about a child's reading.
posts.visual_notes: Text thread. Optional: near point of convergence diagram. Educational, authoritative. Cite JAMA for credibility.

posts.hook: My son was diagnosed with ADHD at 9. Turns out it was convergence insufficiency. Here's what happened.
posts.caption:
r/Parenting or r/optometry

Long post — bear with me because I think this is important.

My son was 9 when his teacher flagged that he couldn't focus during reading time. Pediatrician suggested ADHD evaluation. We got the diagnosis and started medication.

He improved slightly but still struggled with reading specifically. His therapist noticed that he covered one eye while reading. She referred us to a developmental optometrist.

Diagnosis: convergence insufficiency. Near point of convergence of 18cm (normal is 6–10cm).

The standard 20/20 test at his last school screening had been completely normal.

After 14 weeks of vision therapy:
- Near point of convergence: back to 8cm
- Reading fluency improved significantly (tested by school)
- Headaches after school: stopped
- He now actively reads for fun

He's still on a low ADHD dose — we're working with his pediatrician on whether to continue. The convergence insufficiency was definitely a major component.

If your child avoids reading, loses their place constantly, or covers one eye — ask for a near point of convergence test specifically. A standard eye exam will miss this.

The free browser tool I've used for home maintenance exercises: axeo.vision/exercise — convergence section has a guided pencil push-up sequence.
